serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Goreng Pattaya as typically served in a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker centre or restaurant, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice, Malaysian style) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia / FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Goreng Pattaya is a variant of Malaysian nasi goreng (fried rice) wrapped in a thin egg omelette. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD nasi goreng entries and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice data. The egg omelette wrapper contributes additional protein and fat; values adjusted upward accordingly. No exact Goreng Pattaya entry exists in the primary source database.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (white rice, stir-fried with egg and soy sauce)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Goreng Pattaya exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is derived from the closest analogous entry: white fried rice with egg (GI ~64, medium category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (64 × 82.25 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 52.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Goreng Pattaya to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce, as a single serving can provide approximately 64% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving (~52.9)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Goreng Pattaya in small portions due to its medium GI and high glycemic load per standard serving; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion size to approximately 150–200 g can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from soy and chilli sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Goreng Pattaya should be consumed in moderation; the combination of cooking oil, egg, and soy-based sauces contributes to saturated fat and sodium intake, both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goreng Pattaya, also known as Nasi Goreng Pattaya or Pattaya Fried Rice, is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean hawker dish consisting of seasoned fried rice enclosed within a thin folded egg omelette. The dish is prepared by first stir-frying cooked rice in a hot wok with garlic, onion, chicken pieces, soy sauce, and chilli sauce, then pouring beaten egg over the rice and folding the omelette around it to form a neat parcel. The exterior is a soft, lightly golden egg wrap, while the interior contains fragrant, savoury fried rice with a slightly spicy and umami-rich flavour profile. The dish is typically served with sliced cucumber, tomato, and a drizzle of chilli or tomato sauce on top. Goreng Pattaya is a staple at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and Indian-Muslim (mamak) restaurants. It is considered a comfort food and is commonly eaten as a main meal at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from egg and chicken, and carbohydrate predominantly from white rice. The sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and chilli sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. Its medium glycemic index and high glycemic load per serving require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal and dairy-free but contains egg and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact Goreng Pattaya entry was found in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from Malaysian nasi goreng (fried rice) entries with upward adjustment for the egg omelette wrapper. GI is estimated from the closest analogous fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Goreng Pattaya is a Malaysian-origin dish widely served in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD nasi goreng data and FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice composition, with adjustment for the egg omelette component. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food category. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white fried rice with egg from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Goreng Pattaya exists. Sodium content is notably high due to soy sauce and chilli sauce used in preparation. The dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine but is a staple of the Singaporean hawker food landscape, particularly at Indian-Muslim (mamak) stalls. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the dish's strong presence in Singapore's food culture.
